query: association between FHF2 gene variants and encephalopathy

retrieved_docs.title: Alpers-Huttenlocher Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for patients with Alpers-Huttenlocher syndrome is poor, with patients surviving less than 4 years after the initial presentation of symptoms. Common causes of death are fatal encephalopathy and liver failure. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Lafora Disease -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: A case study collected Lafora disease cases for diagnosis and confirmed the genetic spectrum of a family. Furthermore, whole-exome sequencing of the pedigree was conducted for molecular diagnosis. Results showed that a novel biallelic compound heterozygous c.333dupC chr6-18122504 (p.[Gly112ArgfsTer44]) and c.612dupT chr6-18122225 (p.[Gly205Trpfs*29]) mutation in the NHLRC1 gene was elucidated in the progressive myoclonic epilepsy of Lafora pedigree. This means that genetic analysis was helpful in the diagnosis of Lafora disease. Moreover, genetic analysis with alternative tissue biopsy was recommended for patients and close relatives. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Lafora Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Lafora disease is a neurodegenerative disease with autosomal recessive inheritance. Heterozygotes are asymptomatic. EPM2A on chromosome 6q24.3 and EPM2B (ie,  NHLRC1 ) on chromosome 6p22.3 are the known underlying genes leading to deficiencies in laforin and malin, respectively. They seem to contribute almost equally to the pathogenic variants. Most pathogenic variants have mutations resulting in a loss of function, including splice site mutations, missense mutations, nonsense deletions, small intragenic deletions, and insertion mutations. [5] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Fahr Syndrome -- History and Physical -- Clinical Features
retrieved_docs.content: In a study done by Batla et al., a clinical correlation with genomic abnormalities (genotype-phenotype relationship) was established after reviewing 137 cases. Those with SLC20A2 mutations were more commonly observed to have parkinsonism and involvement of the thalamus and dentate nucleus. Those with PDGFB mutations were observed to have a headache more commonly and cysts in white matter. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetic Hearing Loss -- Pathophysiology -- X-Linked Recessive Syndromic Hearing Loss
retrieved_docs.content: Mitochondrial-Inherited syndromic hearing loss often presents with bilateral, high-frequency hearing loss. Syndromes include MELAS syndrome (mitochondria encephalopathy, lactic acidosis, and stroke-like episodes), Kearns-Sayre syndrome, and MERRF (myoclonic epilepsy with ragged red fibers).

retrieved_docs.title: Reversible Cerebral Vasoconstriction Syndromes -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Although definitive genetic risk factors for RCVS have not been conclusively identified, occasionally, this condition has been observed in patients with underlying genetic diseases, such as adult-onset Leigh syndrome. [7] A single study reported that the Val66Met polymorphism in brain-derived neurotrophic factors may influence RCVS, with patients carrying the Val allele showing higher mean vasoconstriction scores across all arterial segments than MetMet homozygotes. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemiplegic Migraine -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: FHM2 is associated with mutations in the ATP1A2 gene on chromosome 1q23. This gene is responsible for encoding a catalytic subunit of sodium/potassium ATPase. [12] The penetrance of ATP1A2 variants ranges from 63 to 87%. [8] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Friedreich Ataxia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Gene therapy is considered the best chance of altering the disease course.

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia (HHT) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Pathogenesis of HHT is primarily due to the gene mutations (endoglin, ACVRL1, and SMAD4) that affect the endothelial cell receptors of the TGF-B superfamily. A mutation in these receptors prevents downstream signaling and disrupts angiogenesis, promoting a disorganized cytoskeleton and dysfunctional remodeling of the vascular endothelium. As a result, these vessels lose elasticity and remain chronically dilated. [18] The loss of vascular integrity, combined with constant pressure, results in telangiectasias (dilated microvessels) and large AVMs. [19] [9] Due to the vessel walls' decreased elasticity and vascular lumen dilation, telangiectasias are fragile and more prone to hemorrhage. AVMs can form in the brain, lungs, GI tract, spine, or liver. [19] Up to 10% of HHT patients have cerebral AVMs, 15 to 45% develop pulmonary AVMs, and 75% have hepatic AVMs. Rupture of these AVMs can result in severe complications, including internal hemorrhage, embolic or hemorrhagic stroke, seizures, migraines, or brain abscesses. [17] Untreated pulmonary and hepatic AVMs can lead to arteriovenous shunting and pulmonary hypertension. [6] . There are phenotypic variations between HHT1 and HHT2. HHT1 patients develop epistaxis earlier in life and have pulmonary AVMs, while HHT2 patients are likelier to develop hepatic AVMs. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Friedreich Ataxia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic testing is the cornerstone of the evaluation of patients with FA.

retrieved_docs.title: Migraine Headache -- Etiology -- Hereditary endotheliopathy with retinopathy, nephropathy, and stroke
retrieved_docs.content: HERNS is a cerebroretinal vasculopathy linked to a locus on chromosome 3p21.

retrieved_docs.title: Fahr Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: There are four genes which have been implicated as the molecular basis of Fahr disease i.e., loss of function mutation in the gene SLC20A2 encoding type 3 sodium-dependent phosphate transporter 2 (PiT2) on chromosome 8p (40%), a mutation in gene XPR1 that encodes for a retroviral receptor with phosphate export function on chromosome 1q (2%), a mutation in the gene which encodes the receptor for members of the platelet-derived growth factor family- gene PDGFRB on chromosome 5q (2%), and gene PDGFB on chromosome 22q (11%). 46% of cases have some unknown gene mutations. Other loci that have been linked to Fahr disease include IBGC1 locus at chromosome 14q, a locus at chromosome 2q, and another one at chromosome 8. [3] Since, some genes have been implicated in Fahr disease, the term idiopathic basal ganglia calcification, used previously, should be avoided.

retrieved_docs.title: Frontotemporal Lobe Dementia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Frontotemporal dementia is mainly a sporadic disease. Genetics also plays a key role where approximately 40% of cases are familial in origin. [7] Among them, 13.4% of cases have an autosomal dominant inheritance. Mutations in over 20 genes have been identified in the possible development of FTD. Commonly involved genes are microtubules associated protein tau located on chromosome 17q21.32, granulin on chromosome 17q21.32, and hexanucleotide repeat expansion of the chromosome 9 open-reading-frame 72 (C9orf72) on chromosomal location 9p21.2 (responsible for cytoskeleton organization). [8] [9] [10] It is also postulated that C9orf72 gene mutation is a continuum between FTD and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis and is responsible for the occurrence of both entities. Head trauma and thyroid disease have been linked with the development of FTD with a 3.3-fold higher risk and 2.5-fold higher risk, respectively.

retrieved_docs.title: Idiopathic (Genetic) Generalized Epilepsy -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Seizures and epilepsy have signnificant associated morbidity. Recurrent seizures are associated with a 12-month follow-up of 40% to 60% risk of bodily injury such as burns, fractures, and concussions. This shows the importance of adequate seizure control. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Fahr Syndrome -- Treatment / Management -- (D) Genetic Counseling
retrieved_docs.content: There are no demonstrated medical benefits for screening the relatives. Predictive testing in the form of brain CT scan (calcium deposits precede clinical manifestations by years) and molecular testing (if PDGFB, PDGFRB, XPR1, SLC20A2 pathogenic variant has been identified in proband) can be offered to asymptomatic first-degree relatives (>18 years) after weighing the ethical and psychosocial conditions because no curative treatment is available. Testing individuals >18 years can help them in decision making in regards to their marriage, family planning, career, and finance. Testing is usually not recommended in individuals <18 years considering social issues, discrimination and anxiety in future, negative impact on parent-child or sibling-child relationship, and uncertain CT scan predictability as penetrance is age-dependent.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Central Nervous System, Malformations -- Clinical Significance -- Periventricular Heterotopia
retrieved_docs.content: This pathology is a genetic disease due to a failure of neurons to migrate, leading to abnormally located nodules around the ventricles. The most common clinical manifestation is an afebrile seizure. Research has shown it to occur alongside other conditions like EDS, Williams syndrome, and Ci du Chat. However, mutations in the filamin A (FLNA (Xq28) and ADP ribosylation factor guanine nucleotide exchange factor 2 (ARFGEF2 (20q13). X-linked FLNA while the ARFGEF2 autosomal recessive. [27] [28] [29]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2198
retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary disorder of nervous system is a broad category of genetic conditions affecting the nervous system, and شقيقة فالجية عائلية (familial hemiplegic migraine) is a specific instance within this category. شقيقة فالجية عائلية represents a hereditary form of migraine that is characterized by transient hemiplegia and other neurological symptoms, illustrating how a hereditary disorder of the nervous system can manifest as a distinct migraine subtype.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1062
retrieved_docs.content: within nervous system disorders. Clinically, شقيقة and شقيقة فالجية عائلية are considered clinically similar, and شقيقة and صداع وعائي are described as related and possibly synonymous, suggesting substantial overlap in their clinical presentation and underlying mechanisms. Together, these relationships integrate migraine into a network of genetic, vascular, and systemic neurological conditions, underscore its diagnostic and therapeutic considerations, and illustrate its connections to both broader headache classifications and specific hereditary migraine phenotypes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 930
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral arteriopathy, autosomal dominant, with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy, type 1 is a hereditary cerebrovascular disorder that characteristically presents with a gait disorder (اضطراب المِشْيَة). The disease’s underlying vascular pathology leads to subcortical infarctions and diffuse white‑matter changes, which together disrupt motor pathways and result in abnormal walking patterns. Recognizing gait disturbance as a key manifestation of this specific type of cerebral arteriopathy aids clinicians in diagnosing the condition and guiding appropriate genetic counseling and management strategies.

An eye‑related subtype, “الشقيقة الشالة للعين,” is recorded as a child of شقيقة and as a descendant of the cranial‑nerve disorder “اضطراب بالعصب القحفي,” reflecting a specific manifestation that involves ocular symptoms. Genetic and hereditary forms are captured by “Hereditary disorder of nervous system,” which is inversely related to the familial migraine entity “شقيقة فالجية عائلية.” These two migraine variants are noted as clinically similar, underscoring their shared pathophysiology despite possible differences in inheritance patterns. In the context of cerebrovascular disease, the rare condition “CEREBRAL ARTERIOPATHY, AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT, WITH SUBCORTICAL INFARCTS AND LEUKOENCEPHALOPATHY, TYPE 1” lists شقيقة as one of its manifestations, linking migraine to subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy. retrieved_docs.content: Other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions are linked to the presence of diffuse encephalitis around the axis, as indicated by الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور, suggesting that these conditions may involve or utilize this widespread inflammatory process in the brain.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2802
retrieved_docs.content: Alzheimer‑type dementia, glandular disease, and hyperthyroidism are all conditions that can be influenced by genetic factors. Genetic predisposition is recognized as an important contributor to the development of Alzheimer‑type dementia, affecting the risk and progression of this neurodegenerative disorder. Similarly, certain glandular diseases have hereditary components that may affect gland function and pathology. Excess thyroid activity, or hyperthyroidism, also exhibits a genetic basis, with inherited variations influencing susceptibility to thyroid hormone overproduction. Understanding the genetic underpinnings of these disorders aids in risk assessment, early detection, and the development of personalized therapeutic strategies.

Both شقيقة and شقيقة فالجية عائلية share clinical features and may be considered synonymous or closely related within the vascular headache spectrum. This underscores the importance of recognizing migraine as a possible neurological manifestation of systemic vascular disorders. retrieved_docs.content: The nervous system (nervový systém) serves as the primary anatomic site associated with other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions. These hereditary and degenerative conditions are linked to the diffuse encephalitis around the axis (الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور), suggesting that this inflammatory process may play a role in their pathophysiology or clinical presentation. Neurosurgery (جراحة الأعصاب) has a broader relationship with the nervous system, providing the specialized surgical expertise needed to diagnose, manage, and treat both the hereditary/degenerative disorders and the associated diffuse encephalitis. Recognizing the interconnections among the nervous system, hereditary and degenerative conditions, the specific encephalitic process, and neurosurgical intervention is essential for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 194
retrieved_docs.content: Migraine (شقيقة) is represented in the knowledge base as a specific nervous‑system disorder that resides within a hierarchy of headache and neurological conditions, being a child of broader entities such as “Headache; including migraine,” “Migraine and Headache Syndromes,” and the general category “اضطراب الجهاز العصبي” (nervous‑system disorder). It is also classified as a type of vascular headache (صداع وعائي) and is closely related to, and sometimes synonymous with, that category. Familial hemiplegic migraine (شقيقة فالجية عائلية) is recorded as a hereditary form of the disorder, linking migraine to genetic and hereditary nervous‑system diseases. Clinically, migraine may appear as a manifestation of other neurological diseases, for example in cerebral arteriopathy, autosomal dominant, with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy (type 1), where it co‑occurs with gait disturbance. Patient histories noting “H/O: migraine” are mapped to personal histories of nervous‑system and

Sodium valproate (valproát sodný) has evidence supporting its use for migraine prophylaxis. Cerebral arteriopathy, autosomal dominant with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy type 1, illustrates the overlap between vascular cerebral disease, gait disorder, and migraine, emphasizing the interconnected nature of vascular, headache, and neurodegenerative processes. Overall, the community’s knowledge highlights the anatomical sites (brain, ventricles, nervous system), clinical associations (dementia, ischemic complications, cranial nerve dysfunction, gait disturbance), genetic and familial forms, and the role of neurosurgical procedures (extracranial ventricular shunting, nasopharyngeal diversion) and pharmacologic prevention (sodium valproate) in integrated diagnosis and management of these interrelated neurological conditions.
